nuggets.content: Though the 2023 third-rounder has shown glimpses of strong play over the last two seasons, the Browns picked their perimeter wide receiver of the future in the second round in Denzel Boston. And though the Browns are committed — “stuck” might be the better term — to Jerry Jeudy for at least one more season and at least somewhat committed to developing 2025 rookie Isaiah Bond, Tillman is entering the final year of his rookie contract and now feels like a summertime trade candidate. Maybe that’s a good thing for Tillman’s career, but he’s on the roster bubble.

The Browns didn’t add to the running back room, and with Quinshon Judkins still rehabbing from the multiple injuries that ended his rookie season, Sanders gets the spring to show why he should be in the team’s plans. Dylan Sampson remains the No. 2 running back on the depth chart but is probably too small to be anything more than a situational player, so Sanders — claimed last August after signing with the Los Angeles Chargers as an undrafted rookie — should have the opportunity to win a role as a true backup and player worth further development.

Sanders prefers to go by Rocket, and the Browns seem thrilled to have scored former Toledo Rocket Emmanuel McNeil-Warren late in the second round after they considered the safety a borderline first-round pick. So it’s just a big weekend for Rockets and a Browns offense that, based on the last two seasons, would seem like a rocket ship if it could get to even 19 points per game in the season ahead.

There’s an open competition here for the Patriots’ No. 3 job behind Stevenson and Henderson. Miller has the straight-line speed that makes him enticing (and potentially the favorite) for the role. But he has struggled in pass protection and as a receiver, two of the top qualities that offensive coordinator Josh McDaniels values in a third-down back.

Heidenreich is a better athlete than his draft position suggests, as Brugler gave the Navy product a fifth-round grade. It doesn’t help the local product that the Steelers had already spent a fourth-round pick on Iowa return specialist Kaden Wetjen. But Heidenreich has beaten the odds before, going from a 0-star recruit to Navy’s all-time leading receiver. It will take some creativity for the Steelers to find a way to utilize Heidenreich, who has some versatility and special-teams value. He surely will have plenty of hometown fans cheering for him in the preseason and at training camp.


Heidenreich will start at the bottom and need to work his way up the depth chart the hard way. He could be a practice squad candidate in the long run, given the Steelers don’t have many roster spots available and filled the need at return man with Wetjen.

He's essentially a big wide receiver. Don't get me wrong, he can be physical and offer some blocking out of the slot, but watching him for three days during the Senior Bowl practices back in January, Joly looked like an athletic big-bodied wide receiver running drills with his more traditional-looking tight-end brethren.


Joly indicated the Broncos believe that he’s a ‘Swiss Army Knife’ and can do a lot of things as an in-line blocker, in the slot, and in the backfield.
